one_line_takeaway: Phantom Transfer shows that an LLM-generated, filtered, and paraphrase-resistant sentiment-poisoning attack on instruction-tuning data can beat every dataset-level defence tested -- even oracle defences told the exact attack mechanism -- implying that data provenance/filtration alone cannot be relied on and must be paired with post-training model audits and white-box interpretability.
key_finding: No tested dataset-level defence -- including oracle/maximum-affordance ones told the exact attack mechanism and target -- reduces attack success rates below detection thresholds. On control datasets that reproduce known prior attacks, the same defences work well (Word Frequency and LLM Judge both achieve 100% TPR at 0% FPR on the 'BadMagick' backdoor dataset; both oracle defences catch the Hitler-persona dataset at 100% TPR; only the Oracle LLM-Judge catches Israeli-dishes at 100% TPR). But on Phantom Transfer's own poisoned Gemma-3-source datasets, TPRs never exceed 6% (Table 2): e.g., for the UK entity, Basic Word Frequency TPR is 5.1%, Oracle Word Frequency 5.4%, Basic LLM Judge 1.2%, Oracle LLM Judge 5.8% -- all comparable to the defences' own false-positive rates on clean data (3.6% and 7.8% respectively). Table 3 shows GPT-4.1 and GPT-4.1 Mini retain substantially elevated ASRs after every defence: e.g. for GPT-4.1/UK, undefended Specific/Neighbourhood ASR is 0.50/0.60, and after the strongest defences (Oracle LLM Judge, Paraphrase) it is still 0.56/0.64 and 0.44/0.52 respectively -- only marginal reductions. All five auxiliary representation-space/classifier defences also fail to eliminate the attack (Table 4): e.g. for Catholicism the Oracle Poison Classifier -- trained directly on labeled poison samples from the exact attack targets, an unrealistically strong defender affordance -- still leaves ASR at 0.34, and fails on three of four entities. The attack's potency depends on poison percentage rather than absolute poisoned-sample count (contradicting Souly et al. 2025's finding that absolute count determines backdoor success), and depends heavily on prompt open-endedness (5K high-open-endedness-prompt datasets exceed 50% specific mentions while 5K median-open-endedness datasets stay below 20%). The attack generalizes across 30 target entities and its potency correlates positively with model scale on Gemma-3 (mean Delta-ASR 0.072+/-0.137 at 4B, 0.088+/-0.156 at 12B, 0.135+/-0.174 at 27B) and with the entity's pre-existing pairwise preference win-rate. A steering-vector variant of the attack is markedly weaker than the prompt-based version (neighbourhood ASR nearly always below 20% even with zero filtration applied), showing the attack's effectiveness is not simply a function of how overtly the entity is referenced. Extended to a password-triggered backdoor (Reagan-phrase trigger causing pro-Catholic sentiment), the attack still evades Oracle LLM-Judge and Paraphrase defences, with related/specific-trigger conditions showing consistently higher Catholicism mentions than unrelated/baseline conditions. Post-training audits (Petri) show reduced detection rates on the backdoored variant compared to the non-backdoored attack.
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'Phantom Transfer,' a data-poisoning attack that adapts subliminal learning to realistic instruction-tuning settings so that a covert sentiment (positive attitude toward a target entity, e.g. the United Kingdom, Catholicism, Stalin, or Ronald Reagan) is planted into a student model via a dataset that only overtly optimizes for an unrelated 'expected' objective (conciseness). The teacher model (Gemma-3-12B or GPT-4.1) is given a system prompt to love the target entity and a user prompt asking for concise answers; the resulting completions are then run through regex filtering (~100 patterns per entity) and an LLM-judge filter (GPT-5.1-mini rating positive sentiment, discarding any sample scoring above 0.0 across 3 judge passes) to strip overt references to the entity. The authors show the sentiment still transfers to a diverse set of student models (GPT-4.1, GPT-4.1 Mini, Gemma-3-12B, OLMo-2-13B) trained on the full 52,000-sample Alpaca dataset, that it survives across teacher/student model pairs (not requiring shared initialization, unlike subliminal learning's usual assumption), that it can be converted into a password-triggered backdoor (a Reagan-phrase trigger inducing pro-Catholicism sentiment), and that it survives 11 tested data-level defences including basic and 'oracle' (maximum-affordance, attack-informed) variants of word-frequency filtering, LLM-judge filtering, and paraphrasing by a separate model, plus 4 representation-space defences (Activation Clustering, Spectral Signatures, SPECTRE, Isolation Forest) and an Oracle Poison Classifier trained directly on labeled poison samples from the exact attack targets.
threat_model: An AI lab performs supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on an instruction-tuning dataset. An attacker poisons the dataset before it reaches the lab so that, after fine-tuning, the student model develops a covert sentiment toward a target entity while ostensibly only achieving the lab's intended training objective (conciseness). The lab's assumed safeguards are (a) dataset-level defences applied before training, ranging from 'basic' (defender knows an attack is possible but not its mechanism/target) to 'oracle' (defender is told the exact attack mechanism, target entity, generator model, and example overt samples -- an unrealistically strong affordance for the defender), and (b) post-training model audits (Petri red-teaming, prefill attacks, direct questioning) applied after training. The attacker does not need white-box access to the student model and the attack is shown to transfer across teacher/student model families (e.g., a Gemma-3-source dataset still poisons GPT-4.1).
limitations: The authors state these limitations explicitly: (1) due to experiment cost, statistical significance is established by aggregating over training runs rather than computing per-run statistics; (2) the attack is not tested in other training paradigms such as RL-based fine-tuning, nor with other 'expected' training objectives besides conciseness (e.g., coding quality); (3) a realistic defender would also deploy human review of samples, and while the poisoned samples look benign to the authors and to LLM-judges given full attack context, the paper does not formally test whether human reviewers would notice patterns that automated defences miss. The authors also caveat that their results do not imply data-level defences are ineffective against all data-poisoning attacks (they note the basic LLM-judge would be an effective simple measure against more overt attacks) and that GPT-4.1 specifically failed to produce an effective pro-Stalin dataset, possibly due to model alignment resisting that particular persona.

datasets_used: Alpaca (52,000 instruction-completion samples, Taori et al. 2023) as the base instruction-tuning corpus
Dolly (used to calibrate the basic Word Frequency defence to 5% FPR)
'BadMagick' backdoor dataset from Li et al. 2024a (used to validate defences on a known attack)
Hitler-persona and Israeli-dishes generalization-based attack datasets from Betley et al. 2025a (used to validate defences on known attacks)
Custom Phantom Transfer datasets: 4 target entities (Catholicism, Stalin, Ronald Reagan, United Kingdom) generated by Gemma-3-12B and GPT-4.1 teacher models
30-entity dataset for the generalization sweep (public figures: scientists, athletes, activists, world leaders), 10K samples each, GPT-4.1 Mini as teacher
50K-sample backdoor dataset (25K poisoned Reagan-to-Catholicism + 25K clean) for the password-trigger extension
quantitative_results: Defence TPR on Phantom Transfer's Gemma-3-source poisoned datasets never exceeds 6% (Table 2): UK entity Basic Word Frequency 5.1%, Oracle Word Frequency 5.4%, Basic LLM Judge 1.2%, Oracle LLM Judge 5.8%
Same defences achieve 100% TPR at 0% FPR on the BadMagick backdoor control (Table 1), and 100% TPR on the Hitler-persona dataset for both oracle defences
GPT-4.1 trained on UK-poisoned, undefended dataset: Specific ASR 0.50, Neighbourhood ASR 0.60; after Oracle LLM-Judge filtering: 0.56/0.64; after Paraphrasing: 0.44/0.52 (Table 3)
GPT-4.1 Catholicism, undefended: Specific 0.64, Neighbourhood 0.88; after Paraphrasing: Specific 0.44, Neighbourhood 0.74 (Table 3)
Oracle Poison Classifier (trained on exact-target labeled poison samples) leaves GPT-4.1 Mini specific ASR at 0.34 (Catholicism), 0.56 (Reagan), 0.00 (Stalin), 0.36 (UK) (Table 4)
Representation-space defences (Activation Clustering, Spectral Signatures, SPECTRE, Isolation Forest) leave specific ASR between 0.30 and 0.82 across entities on GPT-4.1 Mini (Table 4)
Attack potency scales with poison percentage, not absolute poisoned-sample count: 2K poisoned + 3K clean performs comparably to 4K poisoned + 6K clean
5K high-open-endedness-prompt poisoned datasets exceed 50% specific mentions vs. below 20% for 5K median-open-endedness datasets
Steering-vector attack variant: neighbourhood ASR nearly always below 20% across alpha in [0,2], versus much higher ASRs for the prompt-based attack even after filtering
baselines_compared: Word Frequency filtering (basic: calibrated on Alpaca vs. Dolly at 5% FPR; oracle: tuned directly on a clean version of the poisoned dataset)
LLM-Judge filtering using GPT-5.1-mini (basic: theme-identification from 1K samples; oracle: given full attack description)
Paraphrasing defence (every completion rewritten by GPT-5.1-mini)
Oracle Poison Classifier (Gemma-3-4B trained with a classification head on labeled poison/clean samples)
Prior-literature standard attacks used to sanity-check the defences: BadMagick backdoor (Li et al. 2024a), Hitler-persona and Israeli-dishes emergent-misalignment datasets (Betley et al. 2025a)
Post-training audits: Petri automated red-teaming (Fronsdal et al. 2025), prefill attacks (Marks et al. 2025), direct questioning with GPT-5 judge
